metadata.summary: Identifies parties, maps them on a 2x2 influence-vs-impact matrix, documents needs and concerns, plans engagement per quadrant. Register stored as YAML in git; diffs become the relationship history.

content.title: Stakeholder Analysis
content.summary: A six-step methodology that identifies all parties affected by or influencing a change initiative, maps them on a 2x2 influence-vs-impact matrix (Manage Closely / Keep Satisfied / Keep Informed / Monitor), documents individual needs, concerns, and communication preferences, plans engagement cadence per quadrant, and stores the register as a YAML file in git so diffs become the relationship history. The register is a living artifact refreshed at minimum quarterly.

why.p: Requirements gathered from the wrong people are incomplete or conflict-laden; missing a regulator, works council, or downstream API consumer invalidates downstream artifacts. IIBA BABOK v3 ch. 3 treats stakeholder engagement as a Knowledge Area because the cost of a missing stakeholder compounds across every subsequent BA activity. Requiring an `evidence:` field on every attitude assertion prevents the politeness-bias failure where everyone is classified as "supportive."

list.item: Kickoff of any cross-functional initiative with more than five named parties (sponsor, SMEs, end users, regulator, vendor)
Migration/replacement projects where end-user resistance is the dominant risk
Regulated programs (HIPAA, GDPR, SOX, MDR) — the regulator is a mandatory stakeholder
M&A and reorg work where the political map is unstable; rerun the matrix every 2-4 weeks
Pre-RFP/vendor-selection work where the buying committee has hidden influencers (security, procurement, legal)
Solo founder pre-PMF with fewer than three people involved — go directly to customer interviews
Strictly internal engineering refactors with no business stakeholder change — RACI alone is sufficient
Public open-source projects with anonymous community contributors — influence/impact axis is meaningless; use governance patterns
One-off bug fixes or hotfixes — the stakeholder map will be stale before the deploy completes

rule.statement: Always include a hidden-stakeholders review step covering: legal, infosec, procurement, works council/union, accessibility, and downstream API consumers. Missing a stakeholder is the dominant real-world failure mode.
rule.rationale: Hidden stakeholders discovered after elicitation begins trigger scope changes, re-validation, and delayed sign-offs; finding them at step 1 costs a single conversation. BABOK v3 ch. 3 lists stakeholder identification as a Knowledge Area, not a preliminary task.

list.item: Customer — external beneficiary (end users, buyers, partners).
End User — direct solution users (employees, customers).
Sponsor — provides resources and authority (executive, budget owner).
Domain SME — subject matter expert (process owner, specialist, regulator).
Implementation SME — technical expert (developer, architect, integrator).
Tester — validates the solution (QA team, UAT participants).
Regulator — compliance authority (legal, audit, government body).
Supplier — external provider (vendor, partner, outsourcer).

rule.statement: Map every stakeholder to one of four quadrants and set default engagement cadence by quadrant: Manage Closely (HH) = weekly 1:1, Keep Satisfied (HL) = biweekly summary, Keep Informed (LH) = monthly digest, Monitor (LL) = release-only. Override only with explicit rationale.
rule.rationale: Stakeholders managed at the wrong cadence either receive information overload (damaging the relationship) or feel ignored (creating resistance); quadrant-driven defaults calibrate engagement without requiring per-stakeholder judgment for every decision.

rule.statement: Apply the Mitchell-Agle-Wood salience overlay (power × legitimacy × urgency) on top of the matrix for politically sensitive initiatives; flag "definitive" stakeholders (all three) for daily attention.
rule.rationale: The 2x2 matrix collapses multi-dimensional politics into one axis; salience theory adds urgency and legitimacy that explain why some low-title stakeholders have outsized influence.

rule.statement: Require an evidence: field on every attitude assertion (quote, transcript line, ticket link); without evidence, attitude defaults to "unknown," not "0" (neutral).
rule.rationale: Self-reported attitude is unreliable; people say "supportive" in workshops and undermine in private. Evidence-backed attitude claims are actionable; ungrounded attitude claims are noise that produces wrong engagement strategies.

rule.statement: Triangulate self-reported attitude with at least one behavioral signal (meeting attendance, Slack response time, sponsor escalations, ticket comments) before assigning attitude to any high-influence stakeholder.
rule.rationale: High-influence stakeholders whose actual attitude diverges from stated attitude are the most dangerous source of late-stage resistance; early triangulation enables proactive management.

rule.statement: When two stakeholders have conflicting needs, follow this sequence: understand each position (why, not what) → find common ground → identify trade-offs → escalate to sponsor with options → document decision with rationale.
rule.rationale: Conflicts resolved by position-trading ("you get requirement A, I get requirement B") without understanding underlying interests re-emerge during design and testing. Interest-based resolution produces durable agreements.

list.item: Facilitated workshop — for groups with multiple conflicting positions.
One-on-one discussions — for sensitive interpersonal conflicts or political situations.
Prototype/demo — to clarify misunderstandings caused by abstract requirements.
Prioritization exercise — to force trade-off choices when all options cannot be delivered.
Sponsor decision — for conflicts that exceed the BA's authority to resolve.

rule.statement: Store the stakeholder register as YAML in git (stakeholders/register.yaml); subagents emit patches, never overwrite the register directly. Each transition (added, attitude shift, escalated) is committed with a one-line rationale.
rule.rationale: git log -- stakeholders/ becomes the relationship history; git blame shows who reclassified whom; CODEOWNERS gates sponsor sign-off on attitude changes. A wiki or spreadsheet cannot provide any of these audit capabilities.

rule.statement: Refresh the register quarterly at minimum; set a calendar trigger that opens an issue assigned to the BA. In M&A and reorg contexts, refresh every 2-4 weeks.
rule.rationale: Stakeholder registers drift in 2-6 weeks in fast-moving organizations; a static register goes stale and becomes wrong, not just old. Automated refresh triggers prevent the register from becoming a liability.

rule.statement: Never publish names + attitudes outside the BA and sponsor; treat the register as confidential. PII regulations (GDPR Art. 9) may apply if attitude implies political opinion or health status.
rule.rationale: Attitude records about named individuals are sensitive personal data; public exposure damages relationships and may create legal liability in GDPR-jurisdiction projects.

example.title: Discovery prompt
example.note: Constrains to real org chart entries, flags unknown attitudes, and surfaces hidden stakeholders as suggestions rather than entries.

example.title: Engagement-plan prompt
example.note: Uses quadrant mapping as default override policy.
example.code: Given the register below, produce the engagement table. Quadrant mapping: HH=Manage Closely (weekly 1:1), HL=Keep Satisfied (biweekly summary), LH=Keep Informed (monthly digest), LL=Monitor (release notes only). Override only with explicit rationale.

antipattern.description: Agent invents plausible-sounding stakeholders ("VP of Product Excellence") not present in the org chart.
antipattern.reason: Hallucinated stakeholders produce engagement plans with no real recipients; always seed with a real org chart export and reject any name not in the source list unless flagged suggested_to_verify.

antipattern.description: Agent over-classifies all stakeholders as "supportive" (politeness bias).
antipattern.reason: LLMs are trained on polite discourse and default to positive sentiment; use a 4-way enum (+/0/-/unknown) with unknown as the default and require evidence for + or -.

antipattern.description: Agent conflates stakeholder with persona ("As a Busy Manager persona...").
antipattern.reason: A stakeholder is a real named human or role with decision power; a persona is a synthesized archetype for design. Conflating them produces engagement plans targeting archetypes, not real people with authority.

antipattern.description: Agent equates influence with seniority — ranks CFO highest influence by default.
antipattern.reason: Influence flows through indirect networks; the CFO's EA may have more influence on calendar decisions than the CFO. Force the agent to consider gatekeepers, information brokers, and informal leaders, not just org chart level.

antipattern.description: Agent bulk-reclassifies 50 stakeholders in a single run.
antipattern.reason: Bulk reclassification of attitude fields is reputationally toxic if wrong; cap agent actions to 5 stakeholders per run and require human review on the diff.

rule.statement: Require human approval at three points: adding a new stakeholder, changing attitude from unknown to positive or negative, and escalating a conflict to sponsor. Agent prepares the patch; BA or sponsor approves the merge.
rule.rationale: Stakeholder relationship decisions have political consequences; automated execution without human sign-off can damage trust, violate confidentiality expectations, and create legal liability.

list.item: The 2x2 influence/impact matrix flattens multi-dimensional politics — power, urgency, and legitimacy (Mitchell-Agle-Wood salience) are collapsed into one axis and important nuance is lost.
Stakeholder lists drift fast (2–6 weeks in fast-moving orgs); a static register goes stale and becomes wrong, not just old. README mistake #5 is the dominant real-world failure.
Hidden stakeholders (works councils, legal, infosec, procurement, downstream consumers of an API) are systematically missed — agents will not invent them unless given org-chart context.
Self-reported attitude is unreliable — people say "supportive" in workshops and undermine in private. The matrix only works if attitude is triangulated against behaviour.
LLM hallucinates plausible-sounding personas ("VP of Product Excellence") that do not exist in the target org; outputs must be validated against an actual directory / org chart.
Cultures vary: in high-context cultures (JP, KR) influence flows through indirect networks the matrix does not represent.

template.purpose: Stakeholder register table with ID, role, category, influence, impact, attitude, engagement columns.
Individual stakeholder profile with characteristics, needs, concerns, communication preferences, engagement history.
Script: validate YAML register and emit Mermaid quadrant chart for influence-vs-impact visualization.
